_meta.description: Curated health-relevant SNP lookup table for DNA raw data import. Forward (+) strand alleles. snpHints connect genetics to supplement recommendation slots. UPDATE when new research warrants adding/revising SNPs — each entry needs gene, genotypes, effect notes, and at least one citation.

rs1801133.strandNote: Minus strand gene. Forward: G(ref)/A(alt). G=C complement, A=T complement.

GA.note: ~65% enzyme activity; mild homocysteine elevation possible with low folate

AA.note: ~30% enzyme activity; likely elevated homocysteine; consider methylfolate over folic acid

GA.text: Your MTHFR C677T (heterozygous) suggests methylfolate (5-MTHF) may be more effective than folic acid

AA.text: Your MTHFR C677T (homozygous) strongly suggests methylfolate (5-MTHF) over folic acid

rs1801131.strandNote: Minus strand gene. Forward: T(ref)/G(alt). T=A complement, G=C complement.
rs1801131.compoundNote: Compound heterozygous with rs1801133 (GA + TG) produces significant methylation impairment

TG.note: Mildly reduced BH4 synthesis; homocysteine usually normal unless compound-het with C677T

GG.note: Reduced BH4; solo effect on homocysteine small in adequate-folate populations — compound-het with C677T is where clinically meaningful impairment appears

TG.text: Your MTHFR A1298C (heterozygous) suggests methylfolate may be more effective than folic acid for BH4 support

GG.text: Your MTHFR A1298C (homozygous) suggests methylfolate and active B vitamins for BH4 pathway support

AA.note: Normal methionine synthase activity

AG.note: Mildly reduced MTR activity; small heterozygous effect on homocysteine in modern meta-analyses

GG.note: Reduced methionine synthase; may elevate homocysteine especially with low B12

AG.text: Your MTR A2756G (heterozygous) suggests active B12 forms (methylcobalamin) to support homocysteine metabolism

GG.text: Your MTR A2756G (homozygous) suggests active B12 forms (methylcobalamin) and adequate folate for homocysteine clearance

AA.note: Normal B12 recycling via MTRR

GG.note: Impaired B12 recycling; can elevate homocysteine especially with low B12/riboflavin

AG.text: Your MTRR A66G (heterozygous) suggests ensuring adequate active B12 and riboflavin (B2)

GG.text: Your MTRR A66G (homozygous) suggests active B12 forms (methylcobalamin) and riboflavin (B2) for B12 recycling

AA.note: Reduced betaine remethylation; folate therapy may be less effective

GA.note: Altered CBS activity; clinical significance debated

AA.note: Altered CBS activity; may affect homocysteine clearance (direction uncertain)

GA.text: Your CBS c.699C>T (heterozygous) suggests monitoring homocysteine and supporting transsulfuration with B6 (P5P)

AA.text: Your CBS c.699C>T (homozygous) suggests active B6 (P5P) and monitoring homocysteine clearance

GA.note: Carrier; usually asymptomatic, mildly elevated ferritin/transferrin sat possible

AA.note: Hereditary hemochromatosis (type 1); progressive iron accumulation; monitor ferritin regularly

GA.text: Your HFE C282Y (carrier) suggests monitoring ferritin and avoiding iron supplementation unless deficiency is confirmed

AA.text: Your HFE C282Y (homozygous) suggests avoiding iron supplementation and iron-fortified foods — regular ferritin monitoring recommended

rs1799945.compoundNote: Compound heterozygous with rs1800562 (CG + GA) causes moderate iron overload risk

GG.note: Mild iron overload possible; significant risk if compound heterozygous with C282Y

CG.text: Your HFE H63D (heterozygous) suggests monitoring iron levels and avoiding unnecessary iron supplementation

GG.text: Your HFE H63D (homozygous) suggests avoiding iron supplementation and monitoring ferritin regularly

rs855791.strandNote: Forward strand: G(ref=Ala736)/A(alt=Val736). Literature often cites T/C (complement).

AA.note: Higher hepcidin → lower iron absorption; risk of iron-deficiency anemia

GG.note: Efficient hepcidin suppression; normal iron absorption

AA.text: Your TMPRSS6 V736A suggests a higher need for dietary iron or iron supplementation due to increased hepcidin

AG.text: Your TMPRSS6 V736A (heterozygous) suggests a mildly higher iron need due to intermediate hepcidin regulation

rs2235321.strandNote: Forward strand: G(ref)/A(alt). Literature often cites C/T (complement).

AA.note: Lower hepcidin; associated with benign microcytic anemia phenotype
~21% higher transferrin, ~24% higher TIBC, ~25% lower transferrin saturation

TT.note: APOE part 1 — combine with rs7412 for haplotype

TC.note: APOE part 1 — one ε4 allele possible

CC.note: APOE part 2 — combine with rs429358 for haplotype

CT.note: APOE part 2 — one ε2 allele possible

GT.note: PCSK9 loss-of-function; ~15% lower LDL, ~25% lower Lp(a); protective

TT.note: Very rare; very low LDL; strong protective effect

rs708272.strandNote: dbSNP forward alleles G/A (= classical B2/B1). Old literature uses G/T (reverse strand) — A allele here is the same as the T allele in older papers.

AA.note: Lowest CETP activity; highest HDL; ~24% lower MI risk per A allele (B1B1)

CT.note: Mildly higher HDL; slightly larger LDL particles

TT.note: Highest HDL; larger, more buoyant LDL particles

rs2282679.strandNote: Forward strand: G(ref)/T(alt). Literature often cites C/A (complement).

GG.note: Normal vitamin D binding protein levels

TT.note: Significantly lower 25(OH)D (~4-8 nmol/L lower); most replicated vitamin D SNP

GT.text: Your GC VDBP (heterozygous) suggests a modestly higher vitamin D3 need due to lower binding protein

TT.text: Your GC VDBP (homozygous) suggests a significantly higher vitamin D3 need — this is the most replicated vitamin D SNP

AA.note: Highest CYP2R1 activity; efficient 25-hydroxylation

AG.text: Your CYP2R1 25-hydroxylase (heterozygous) suggests a mildly higher vitamin D3 need due to reduced conversion

GG.text: Your CYP2R1 25-hydroxylase (homozygous) suggests a higher vitamin D3 need due to reduced 25-hydroxylation

AA.note: Longer VDR isoform; slightly lower receptor efficiency. Downstream 25(OH)D levels largely unaffected per Jolliffe meta-analysis — phenotypic effect on bone density and supplementation response is small.

GG.note: Shorter VDR isoform; more transcriptionally active

AA.text: Your VDR FokI suggests maintaining higher 25(OH)D levels to compensate for lower receptor efficiency

TT.note: Higher 25(OH)D-to-active-form conversion; may compensate for low 25(OH)D

GG.note: Secretor (wild-type); normal gut microbiome composition and intrinsic-factor B12 uptake

GA.note: Heterozygous secretor; intermediate B12 levels, typically clinically unremarkable

AA.note: Non-secretor; higher total B12 (lab artifact from haptocorrin); does not indicate better B12 status — check functional markers (homocysteine, MMA) rather than trusting serum B12

GA.text: Your FUT2 W154X (heterozygous secretor) suggests sublingual B12 forms may offer better bioavailability

AA.note: Normal haptocorrin production

GG.note: Lower haptocorrin; lower total serum B12 (holoTC may be less affected)

CC.note: Normal transcobalamin II; efficient cellular B12 delivery

GG.note: Reduced holotranscobalamin → impaired cellular B12; total serum B12 can be misleadingly normal

CG.text: Your TCN2 P259R (heterozygous) suggests sublingual or hydroxocobalamin B12 for better cellular delivery

GG.text: Your TCN2 P259R (homozygous) suggests sublingual methylcobalamin or hydroxocobalamin — serum B12 may be misleadingly normal

AA.note: Reduced cubilin function; lower dietary B12 absorption

GA.text: Your CUBN cubilin (heterozygous) suggests sublingual B12 forms to partially bypass reduced intestinal absorption

AA.text: Your CUBN cubilin (homozygous) suggests sublingual or injectable B12 — intestinal absorption is significantly reduced

6/7.note: ~75% UGT1A1 activity; mildly elevated unconjugated bilirubin

7/7.note: Gilbert syndrome; UGT1A1 ~30-60% of normal; bilirubin 2-3× elevated; benign but affects drug metabolism

GA.note: Mildly reduced glucuronidation; slightly elevated bilirubin

AA.note: Gilbert syndrome equivalent (common in East Asian populations); markedly elevated bilirubin

CC.note: Normal DIO1; standard peripheral T4→T3 conversion

CT.note: Mildly reduced DIO1; may lower T3/T4 ratio

TT.note: Reduced DIO1; lower T3 production; may need higher T4 dose to achieve target T3

CT.text: Your DIO1 3'-UTR (heterozygous) suggests selenium (selenomethionine) to support T4-to-T3 conversion

TT.text: Your DIO1 3'-UTR (homozygous) suggests selenium and monitoring T3/T4 ratio — peripheral conversion is reduced

TT.note: Normal DIO2; efficient intracellular T4→T3 conversion

CC.note: Impaired tissue T4→T3 conversion; serum T3 may appear normal while intracellular T3 is suboptimal; may benefit from T4+T3 combination therapy

TC.text: Your DIO2 Thr92Ala (heterozygous) suggests selenium and zinc to support intracellular T4-to-T3 conversion

CC.text: Your DIO2 Thr92Ala (homozygous) suggests selenium, zinc, and discussing T4+T3 combination therapy with your doctor

AA.note: Significantly elevated Graves' disease risk (~2-3×); no association with Hashimoto's

rs174546.markerNote: Maps to custom fatty acid markers (omega-3 index, AA, EPA, DHA)

CC.note: High delta-5 desaturase activity; efficient n-3/n-6 PUFA conversion

TT.note: Low D5D activity; higher DGLA, lower AA; poor ALA→EPA/DHA conversion; may need preformed DHA

TC.text: Your FADS1 3'-UTR (heterozygous) suggests preformed EPA/DHA may be more effective than plant-based ALA

TT.text: Your FADS1 3'-UTR (homozygous) suggests preformed DHA from fish or algal oil — ALA conversion is significantly reduced

rs174547.markerNote: In strong LD with rs174546; largely redundant

TT.note: Low D5D; reduced n-3/n-6 conversion efficiency

TC.text: Your FADS1 coding region (heterozygous) suggests preformed EPA/DHA over plant-based omega-3

TT.text: Your FADS1 coding region (homozygous) suggests preformed DHA from fish or algal oil over ALA

rs174575.markerNote: Maps to custom fatty acid markers (GLA, AA, EPA, DHA)

CC.note: Normal D6D; standard LA→GLA and ALA→SDA conversion

GG.note: Reduced D6D; lower GLA/SDA; may benefit from GLA (evening primrose) and preformed EPA/DHA

CG.text: Your FADS2 delta-6 desaturase (heterozygous) suggests preformed EPA/DHA may be more effective

GG.text: Your FADS2 delta-6 desaturase (homozygous) suggests preformed EPA/DHA and possibly GLA (evening primrose oil)

rs953413.markerNote: Maps to custom fatty acid markers (EPA, DHA, Omega-3 Index)

AA.note: Normal ELOVL2; efficient EPA→DHA elongation

GG.note: Reduced elongase; EPA supplementation may not efficiently raise DHA; supplement DHA directly

AG.text: Your ELOVL2 elongase (heterozygous) suggests supplementing DHA directly — EPA elongation may be mildly reduced

GG.text: Your ELOVL2 elongase (homozygous) suggests supplementing DHA directly rather than relying on EPA conversion

CT.note: ~1.5× lifetime T2D risk; mildly impaired insulin secretion. Absolute risk depends heavily on weight, diet, and activity.

TT.note: ~2× lifetime T2D risk; impaired post-prandial insulin release. Absolute risk still depends heavily on weight, diet, and activity — lifestyle matters more than genotype.

CT.text: Your TCF7L2 (heterozygous) is a tendency toward harder blood-sugar control — post-meal walks, lower glycemic load, and berberine can each help

TT.text: Your TCF7L2 (homozygous) is a tendency toward harder blood-sugar control — post-meal walks, lower glycemic load, and berberine are worth trying. Lifestyle matters more than this genotype.

CC.note: Standard PPARγ2; highest insulin resistance risk

CG.note: Improved insulin sensitivity; reduced T2D risk (protective)

GG.note: Best insulin sensitivity; lowest T2D risk (protective)

TG.note: Modestly altered adiponectin; effect on insulin sensitivity is modest and mainly documented in Asian populations

GG.note: Altered adiponectin; insulin-resistance effect is population-stratified — meaningful in Asian cohorts, weak elsewhere

TT.note: Lower adiponectin; higher insulin resistance and fasting insulin

CT.text: Your SHBG promoter (heterozygous) suggests boron (calcium fructoborate) may help modulate SHBG levels

TT.text: Your SHBG promoter (homozygous) suggests lower SHBG — boron and nettle root may help optimize free hormone balance

AA.note: ~24-39% higher SHBG; higher total but lower free testosterone

GA.text: Your SHBG 5'-region (heterozygous) suggests monitoring SHBG — boron may help optimize free hormone levels

AA.text: Your SHBG 5'-region (homozygous) suggests elevated SHBG — boron and stinging nettle root may help optimize free hormone levels

rs743572.strandNote: Forward strand: A(ref)/G(alt). Literature often cites T/C (complement).

AA.note: Normal CYP17A1; baseline sex steroid precursor synthesis

GG.note: Higher steroid synthesis flux; increased estradiol in women, altered T/E ratio in men

rs700518.strandNote: Forward strand: C(ref)/T(alt) — actual Ancestry alleles. Literature cites A/G (complement).

CC.note: Altered testosterone→estradiol conversion; direction depends on sex and body composition
Lower CYP1B1 activity; less genotoxic 4-OH-estradiol production

CG.text: Your CYP1B1 Leu432Val (heterozygous) suggests DIM or calcium D-glucarate to support healthy estrogen metabolism

GG.text: Your CYP1B1 Leu432Val (homozygous) suggests DIM, calcium D-glucarate, and cruciferous vegetables to reduce 4-OH-estradiol
